clinicalStem: A 34-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a sudden onset of severe, colicky right flank pain radiating to the groin and right testicle. He is restless and cannot find a comfortable position. He reports nausea and has vomited twice. On examination, his temperature is 37.2°C, HR 102 bpm, BP 128/82 mmHg. Urinalysis shows haematuria (3+) and no leukocytes. A non-contrast CT KUB confirms a 4 mm calculus at the right vesicoureteric junction. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: IV morphine + IV ketorolac + oral tamsulosin + conservative monitoring
Emergency ureteroscopy and stone extraction
Urgent nephrostomy tube insertion
Extracorporeal shockwave lithotripsy (ESWL) today
Oral co-amoxiclav and outpatient urology referral
explanation: A 4 mm distal ureteric stone has a high rate of spontaneous passage (>80%). Initial management focuses on analgesia — NSAIDs (ketorolac) are first-line for renal colic, combined with opioids if needed. Alpha-blockers such as tamsulosin promote stone passage through smooth muscle relaxation of the distal ureter. There is no sign of infection or obstruction requiring urgent intervention.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: analgesia with NSAID + opioid, alpha-blocker for medical expulsive therapy, and conservative monitoring is appropriate for an uncomplicated distal stone ≤5 mm.
Emergency ureteroscopy is reserved for stones >10 mm, failure of conservative management after 4–6 weeks, or obstructed infected kidney — not required here.
Nephrostomy is indicated for obstructed infected kidney (pyonephrosis) or anuric obstruction, neither of which applies here.
ESWL is an elective procedure for eligible stones and is not performed as an emergency in a stable, uninfected patient.
Antibiotics are not indicated without evidence of urinary tract infection; co-amoxiclav alone does not address pain or stone passage.
clinicalPearl: For distal ureteric stones ≤5 mm without infection or obstruction, NSAIDs + tamsulosin (medical expulsive therapy) and expectant management is first-line.
keyClue: 4 mm stone at the vesicoureteric junction with haematuria but no fever or leukocytes — uncomplicated small distal stone.
commonExamTrap: Choosing emergency ureteroscopy — intervention is not indicated for a small, uncomplicated stone with no signs of sepsis or obstruction.
missedConcept: Medical expulsive therapy with alpha-blockers and the criteria for conservative vs. interventional management of ureteric stones.

clinicalStem: A 58-year-old diabetic woman is brought to the emergency department with a 2-day history of right loin pain, rigors, high fever (39.6°C), and vomiting. She appears flushed and unwell. Observations: HR 118 bpm, BP 96/58 mmHg, RR 24/min, SpO2 95% on air. Urine dipstick shows nitrites+++, leukocytes+++, and blood++. Serum creatinine is 210 µmol/L (baseline 90). Urgent CT KUB shows a 9 mm right proximal ureteric calculus with gross right hydroureteronephrosis and perinephric fat stranding. Blood cultures are sent. What is the single most important next step?
options: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, IV fluid resuscitation, and urgent urological decompression (nephrostomy or ureteric stent)
IV antibiotics alone and repeat bloods in 6 hours
Oral ciprofloxacin and same-day outpatient ureteroscopy
Analgesia, IV fluids, and CT-guided renal abscess drainage
Immediate ureteroscopy and stone extraction under general anaesthesia
explanation: This patient has septic shock secondary to an obstructed infected kidney (pyonephrosis) — a urological emergency. The triad of obstruction, infection, and sepsis requires simultaneous resuscitation (IV fluids, broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour) and urgent drainage (percutaneous nephrostomy or retrograde ureteric stent). Definitive stone treatment is deferred until sepsis is controlled. Antibiotics alone without decompression are insufficient and dangerous.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: IV antibiotics + fluid resuscitation + urgent decompression addresses all limbs of this urological emergency simultaneously.
Antibiotics alone without drainage in an obstructed infected system will not resolve the sepsis source — urine under pressure cannot drain, so antibiotic penetration is inadequate.
Oral antibiotics and elective ureteroscopy are wholly inappropriate in a septic, haemodynamically unstable patient.
There is no renal abscess on CT; perinephric fat stranding indicates infection around the obstructed kidney, not a drainable collection. The primary issue is obstructed collecting system.
Definitive stone extraction under GA is contraindicated in active septic shock — decompression (nephrostomy or stent) must precede definitive stone removal.
clinicalPearl: Obstructed infected kidney = urological emergency: resuscitate AND decompress urgently. Antibiotics alone are insufficient — the pus under pressure must be drained.
keyClue: Septic shock + ureteric calculus + hydroureteronephrosis in a diabetic — classic pyonephrosis requiring immediate decompression.
commonExamTrap: Choosing antibiotics alone — without decompression of the obstructed infected system, source control is not achieved and the patient will deteriorate.
missedConcept: Source control (drainage) is as critical as antibiotics in obstructed infected upper urinary tract — antibiotics cannot penetrate pus under pressure.
redFlagReason: Septic shock secondary to pyonephrosis — failure to decompress urgently risks multi-organ failure and death.

clinicalStem: A 72-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 10-hour history of inability to pass urine, progressive lower abdominal pain, and intense desire to void. He has a background of lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS) and takes no medications. On examination he is in obvious distress. His abdomen shows a palpable, tender, dull suprapubic mass extending to the umbilicus. Observations are otherwise stable. Bladder ultrasound confirms 850 mL of urine. What is the immediate management?
options: Urethral catheterisation and drainage of the bladder
IV furosemide 40 mg to promote diuresis
Oral tamsulosin and review in 4 hours
Urgent cystoscopy to assess for bladder outlet obstruction
Suprapubic catheter insertion as first-line intervention
explanation: Acute urinary retention with 850 mL in the bladder requires immediate decompression via urethral catheterisation. This relieves the patient's pain and prevents further bladder damage, upper tract obstruction, and post-obstructive nephropathy. Furosemide would worsen retention; tamsulosin takes days to weeks to act; cystoscopy is not the first step. Suprapubic catheter is reserved for failed urethral catheterisation (e.g. urethral stricture).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: urethral catheterisation immediately decompresses the acutely distended bladder and relieves pain and pressure.
IV furosemide would increase urine production in an already obstructed bladder, worsening distension and pain — absolutely contraindicated.
Tamsulosin is useful for trial without catheter (TWOC) after initial drainage, but cannot treat established acute retention — the patient cannot void at all.
Cystoscopy is an elective investigation for chronic LUTS workup; it is not appropriate in an acute retention emergency.
Suprapubic catheter is used when urethral catheterisation fails (stricture, false passage) — urethral catheterisation is always attempted first.
clinicalPearl: Acute urinary retention requires immediate urethral catheterisation; alpha-blockers (tamsulosin) are started after drainage to facilitate a trial without catheter.
keyClue: 10-hour inability to void + palpable suprapubic mass + 850 mL on ultrasound = acute urinary retention requiring immediate drainage.
commonExamTrap: Starting tamsulosin without catheterisation — alpha-blockers cannot drain 850 mL acutely and take days to work.
missedConcept: Acute urinary retention is a urological emergency requiring immediate catheterisation; pharmacotherapy alone is insufficient in the acute setting.
redFlagReason: Acute urinary retention with nearly 1 litre in the bladder — untreated causes severe pain, bladder injury, upper tract obstruction, and acute kidney injury.

clinicalStem: A 65-year-old man attends his GP with a 12-month history of urinary hesitancy, poor stream, feeling of incomplete bladder emptying, and nocturia twice per night. He has no haematuria or dysuria. Digital rectal examination reveals a smoothly enlarged, non-tender, non-nodular prostate. PSA is 2.1 ng/mL. Post-void residual urine is 80 mL. IPSS score is 18 (moderate). He has well-controlled hypertension on amlodipine. Which drug is most appropriate as first-line medical therapy for his LUTS?
options: Tamsulosin (alpha-1 blocker)
Finasteride (5-alpha reductase inhibitor)
Oxybutynin (anticholinergic)
Doxazosin (non-selective alpha blocker)
Sildenafil (PDE5 inhibitor)
explanation: Alpha-1 selective blockers such as tamsulosin are first-line for moderate BPH-related LUTS. They relax smooth muscle in the prostate and bladder neck, improving flow within days to weeks. 5-alpha reductase inhibitors (finasteride) are reserved for larger prostates (>30–40 mL) or combined therapy; they take 6 months for maximum effect. Oxybutynin is contraindicated with significant post-void residual as it worsens retention. Non-selective alpha blockers like doxazosin cause significant hypotension and are less preferred.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: tamsulosin is uroselective (alpha-1A subtype), has rapid onset, minimal cardiovascular side effects, and is first-line for moderate BPH LUTS.

Finasteride reduces prostate volume but takes 6 months for full effect and is best for large prostates (>40 mL) or to reduce risk of retention/surgery — not first-line monotherapy for moderate LUTS with a normal-sized prostate.
Oxybutynin (anticholinergic) treats overactive bladder (urgency, frequency) but can precipitate acute retention by reducing detrusor contractility — contraindicated with 80 mL residual.
Doxazosin is a non-selective alpha blocker; it causes more postural hypotension than tamsulosin and is less preferred, especially in a patient already on amlodipine.
Sildenafil (PDE5 inhibitor) has some evidence for LUTS/BPH but is not first-line pharmacotherapy; it is primarily used for erectile dysfunction.
clinicalPearl: Alpha-1 selective blockers (tamsulosin, alfuzosin) are first-line for BPH-related LUTS; add a 5-ARI for large prostates or when preventing progression.
keyClue: Smoothly enlarged prostate, moderate IPSS (18), PSA 2.1 — typical BPH without large prostate volume, ideal for alpha-blocker monotherapy.
commonExamTrap: Choosing finasteride as first-line — it is appropriate only for large prostates (>40 mL) or combined therapy to reduce risk of urinary retention.
missedConcept: The distinction between alpha blockers (rapid symptom relief) and 5-ARIs (disease modification in large glands) and their complementary roles in BPH management.

subtopic: Emergency scrotal pain — diagnosis and management
clinicalStem: A 15-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his parents at 02:00 after waking from sleep with sudden onset of severe left scrotal pain and nausea. He vomited once. On examination, the left testicle is high-riding, firm, and exquisitely tender. The cremasteric reflex is absent on the left. There is no urethral discharge. Urinalysis is normal. What is the single most important next step?
options: Immediate surgical exploration of the scrotum
Urgent scrotal Doppler ultrasound to confirm absent blood flow
IV antibiotics for epididymo-orchitis and reassess
Manual detorsion at bedside and discharge
Urine culture and sexually transmitted infection (STI) swabs
explanation: Testicular torsion is a surgical emergency with a 6-hour window to save the testis (>90% salvage if explored within 6 hours; <10% after 24 hours). The clinical picture — adolescent male, sudden onset, high-riding tender testicle, absent cremasteric reflex — is pathognomonic. Scrotal exploration must NOT be delayed for imaging. Any delay for Doppler ultrasound risks irreversible ischaemia. Bilateral orchidopexy is performed at surgery (fixing both testes to prevent future torsion).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: immediate surgical exploration is the only acceptable management — do not delay for imaging when clinical suspicion is high.

Doppler ultrasound is useful when diagnosis is uncertain, but in a classic presentation (adolescent, high-riding testis, absent cremasteric reflex), imaging must NOT delay theatre. A normal Doppler does not exclude torsion.
Epididymo-orchitis is possible in sexually active adults but rare in adolescents without discharge/STI risk; treating antibiotics and waiting in a boy with absent cremasteric reflex is dangerous.
Manual detorsion (outward rotation) can be attempted as a temporising measure while theatre is prepared, but surgery is still required for fixation — discharge alone is never appropriate.
STI workup is irrelevant in a 15-year-old with a classic torsion presentation; this delay is potentially organ-threatening.
clinicalPearl: Testicular torsion = time-critical surgical emergency. Do NOT wait for Doppler ultrasound. Scrotal exploration within 6 hours saves >90% of testes.
keyClue: Adolescent male, sudden onset at night, high-riding testicle, absent cremasteric reflex — classic testicular torsion until proven otherwise.
commonExamTrap: Ordering Doppler ultrasound first — imaging must never delay surgery in a high-probability torsion presentation.
missedConcept: The cremasteric reflex is the most reliable clinical sign; its absence in an adolescent with acute scrotal pain mandates immediate exploration regardless of imaging.
redFlagReason: Testicular torsion — irreversible ischaemia within 6 hours; delay to imaging or antibiotics risks loss of the testis.

clinicalStem: A 62-year-old man, a lifelong smoker (40 pack-years), presents to his GP with two episodes of painless macroscopic haematuria over the past 3 weeks. He denies dysuria, frequency, or systemic symptoms. Examination is unremarkable. Dipstick confirms blood 3+; urine culture is negative. Renal function is normal. What is the most appropriate next investigation?
options: Urgent flexible cystoscopy and upper tract imaging (CT urogram)
Urine cytology alone and repeat dipstick in 6 weeks
Transrectal ultrasound-guided prostate biopsy
Urine culture and empirical antibiotics for 7 days
Renal ultrasound and PSA only
explanation: Painless macroscopic haematuria in a man over 50 with smoking history is bladder transitional cell carcinoma until proven otherwise and mandates urgent investigation. The gold standard workup is cystoscopy (to visualise the bladder mucosa) plus upper tract imaging (CT urogram) to exclude urothelial carcinoma of the upper tracts and renal cell carcinoma. NICE criteria classify this as a 2-week-wait referral. Urine culture is negative, making infection an unlikely cause.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: visible haematuria in an older smoker warrants urgent cystoscopy + CT urogram to exclude urothelial malignancy at any level of the urinary tract.
Urine cytology has poor sensitivity for low-grade bladder cancer and cannot replace cystoscopy; a 6-week delay in a potential cancer is unacceptable.
Prostate cancer rarely causes macroscopic haematuria; TRUS biopsy is not indicated without elevated PSA or suspicious DRE in this context.
Urine culture is negative — antibiotics treat infection, not haematuria of unknown cause in a high-risk patient.
Renal ultrasound misses flat urothelial lesions in the bladder and upper tracts; PSA is not relevant to macroscopic haematuria workup without prostate symptoms.
clinicalPearl: Painless macroscopic haematuria = bladder cancer until proven otherwise — refer urgently for cystoscopy + CT urogram regardless of urine culture result.
keyClue: Painless macroscopic haematuria + older male + heavy smoker = high-risk for urothelial carcinoma requiring urgent 2-week-wait referral.
commonExamTrap: Treating with antibiotics for 'UTI' when the urine culture is negative — painless haematuria without infection must be investigated for malignancy.
missedConcept: Painless haematuria in a high-risk patient (age >50, smoker) mandates malignancy exclusion; a negative urine culture does not reassure against cancer.

clinicalStem: A 68-year-old man is referred by his GP after a PSA of 18.4 ng/mL was found incidentally on a well-man health check. He is asymptomatic. Digital rectal examination reveals an irregular, hard, non-tender prostate with obliteration of the median sulcus. He has no bone pain or neurological symptoms. Renal function and FBC are normal. What is the most appropriate next investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) of the prostate followed by targeted transrectal or transperineal biopsy
Transrectal ultrasound (TRUS)-guided prostate biopsy without prior imaging
Bone scan to detect metastatic disease
Repeat PSA in 3 months before further investigation
Prostate-specific antigen density (PSAD) calculation and watchful waiting
explanation: Current NICE and EAU guidelines recommend multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) before biopsy for men with suspected prostate cancer. mpMRI risk-stratifies lesions (PI-RADS score), reducing unnecessary biopsies and improving detection of clinically significant cancer. If mpMRI shows a suspicious lesion (PI-RADS ≥3), targeted biopsy is performed. This man has a markedly elevated PSA (18.4) and suspicious DRE — prompt tissue diagnosis is required. A bone scan is appropriate after histological confirmation to stage metastatic disease.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: mpMRI first (PROMIS/PRECISION trial evidence) improves detection of clinically significant prostate cancer and reduces unnecessary biopsies.
TRUS biopsy without prior mpMRI is the older standard; current guidelines prefer MRI-targeted biopsy as it has superior sensitivity for clinically significant cancer.
Bone scan is important for staging once cancer is histologically confirmed, but performing it before tissue diagnosis is premature and puts the cart before the horse.
Repeating PSA in 3 months introduces unacceptable delay in a man with PSA 18.4 and highly suspicious DRE findings — active investigation is required now.
PSAD calculation and watchful waiting are surveillance strategies for low-risk, confirmed low-grade prostate cancer — not appropriate in a diagnostic workup with this PSA and DRE.
clinicalPearl: mpMRI before prostate biopsy (MRI-first pathway) is the current standard — it improves detection of clinically significant cancer and reduces overdiagnosis of indolent disease.
keyClue: PSA 18.4 + irregular hard prostate on DRE — high clinical suspicion for prostate cancer requiring urgent MRI-guided tissue diagnosis.
commonExamTrap: Ordering bone scan before tissue diagnosis — staging investigations follow histological confirmation, not vice versa.
missedConcept: The MRI-first pathway (mpMRI before biopsy) is now standard of care for suspected prostate cancer, replacing blind TRUS biopsy.

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old male ex-smoker presents with a 6-week history of left-sided loin pain and one episode of painless macroscopic haematuria. On examination there is a palpable left flank mass. He has lost 5 kg in 3 months. Blood tests show: Hb 195 g/L (polycythaemia), corrected calcium 2.85 mmol/L (hypercalcaemia), ESR 95 mm/hr. CT of the abdomen shows a 9 cm heterogeneous left renal mass with central necrosis. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Renal cell carcinoma (RCC)
Transitional cell carcinoma of the renal pelvis
Wilms' tumour (nephroblastoma)
Renal abscess
Angiomyolipoma
explanation: The classic triad of haematuria, loin pain, and flank mass (Virchow's triad of RCC) combined with paraneoplastic features — polycythaemia (ectopic EPO production), hypercalcaemia (PTHrP), and elevated ESR — strongly points to renal cell carcinoma. RCC classically presents in middle-aged to older men and is associated with smoking. The large heterogeneous mass with necrosis on CT is characteristic. Up to 30% of RCC cases present with paraneoplastic syndromes.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: RCC presents with the classic haematuria-loin pain-mass triad plus paraneoplastic syndromes (polycythaemia, hypercalcaemia); CT findings are characteristic.

TCC of the renal pelvis causes haematuria but typically presents as a urothelial lesion in the collecting system rather than a large parenchymal mass with necrosis; paraneoplastic polycythaemia is not typical.
Wilms' tumour (nephroblastoma) occurs in children under 5 — this 55-year-old man is entirely outside the typical age range.

Renal abscess causes fever, systemic sepsis, and elevated inflammatory markers but does not produce polycythaemia; CT would show a rim-enhancing fluid collection rather than a solid heterogeneous mass.
Angiomyolipoma contains macroscopic fat visible on CT (Hounsfield units < -20) and is associated with tuberous sclerosis; it does not cause polycythaemia or the described CT findings.
clinicalPearl: RCC = haematuria + loin pain + flank mass (classic triad) + paraneoplastic syndromes (polycythaemia from ectopic EPO, hypercalcaemia from PTHrP).
keyClue: Middle-aged male smoker with the classic RCC triad plus polycythaemia and hypercalcaemia = paraneoplastic RCC until proven otherwise.
commonExamTrap: Missing the paraneoplastic syndromes as diagnostic clues — polycythaemia in a renal mass is ectopic EPO production from RCC.
missedConcept: RCC produces multiple paraneoplastic syndromes (polycythaemia, hypercalcaemia, hypertension, Stauffer syndrome) that can be the presenting features before metastasis is detected.

clinicalStem: A 26-year-old man notices a painless lump in his right testicle 3 weeks ago that has not resolved. He has no history of trauma or infection. He denies fever or dysuria. On examination there is a firm, non-tender, 2 cm nodule on the right testis that does not transilluminate. The left testis is normal. What is the most appropriate immediate investigation?
options: Scrotal ultrasound
CT abdomen and pelvis for staging
Tumour markers (AFP, beta-hCG, LDH) only and discharge
Surgical excision biopsy of the nodule through a scrotal incision
Reassurance and review in 4 weeks
explanation: A firm, painless, non-transilluminant testicular mass in a young man is testicular cancer until proven otherwise and mandates urgent scrotal ultrasound as the first investigation. Ultrasound differentiates testicular from epididymal lesions and identifies malignant characteristics (hypoechoic, vascular). Tumour markers (AFP, beta-hCG, LDH) are measured but a normal result does not exclude malignancy. CT staging and radical orchidectomy via an inguinal incision follow after ultrasound confirmation — scrotal incision must be avoided to prevent lymphatic seeding.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: scrotal ultrasound is the immediate first-line investigation for any testicular mass to characterise the lesion and guide urgent referral.
CT staging is appropriate after ultrasound confirms a suspicious testicular lesion — performing CT before ultrasound skips the diagnostic step.
Tumour markers alone cannot diagnose testicular cancer; 15–25% of seminomas and some non-seminomas have normal markers. Discharge without imaging is dangerous.
Scrotal biopsy/excision via a scrotal incision is absolutely contraindicated — it risks seeding tumour to inguinal lymphatics and altering staging. The correct approach is inguinal orchidectomy.
Reassurance is never appropriate for a firm, non-transilluminant testicular mass in a young man — malignancy must be excluded urgently.
clinicalPearl: Any solid testicular mass = urgent scrotal ultrasound; if malignant, radical orchidectomy via inguinal incision — NEVER transcrotal biopsy (risks lymphatic seeding).
keyClue: Young man + firm painless testicular nodule + does not transilluminate = testicular cancer until proven otherwise.
commonExamTrap: Performing biopsy via scrotal incision — this is absolutely contraindicated; inguinal orchidectomy is the diagnostic and therapeutic procedure.
missedConcept: Scrotal incision for testicular tumours violates the lymphatic drainage barrier, upstaging disease and worsening prognosis — inguinal orchidectomy is mandatory.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman presents with a 3-day history of rigors, fever (38.9°C), right loin pain, nausea, and vomiting. She has dysuria and frequency. On examination she is tender in the right renal angle. Observations: HR 105 bpm, BP 108/72 mmHg, RR 22/min. Urinalysis: leukocytes 3+, nitrites 2+, blood 1+. Blood cultures are taken. Urine culture is pending. Serum creatinine is mildly elevated at 115 µmol/L. A renal ultrasound shows no obstruction and no abscess. What is the most appropriate management?
options: IV cephalosporin (e.g. cefuroxime) and IV fluid resuscitation pending culture results
Oral nitrofurantoin for 7 days and outpatient management
Oral trimethoprim and review in 48 hours
Urgent ureteric stent insertion
CT urogram to exclude renal calculus before starting antibiotics
explanation: This patient has acute pyelonephritis with signs of systemic sepsis (fever, tachycardia, tachypnoea, borderline hypotension, mildly elevated creatinine). She requires hospital admission, IV fluid resuscitation, and IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (cephalosporin or co-amoxiclav) initiated promptly before culture results return. Oral antibiotics are appropriate only in mild, non-systemic pyelonephritis. Ultrasound has excluded obstruction. Nitrofurantoin does not achieve adequate tissue/renal parenchymal levels and must not be used for upper UTI.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: IV antibiotics and fluids for pyelonephritis with systemic features — treatment cannot wait for culture results; de-escalate once sensitivities are known.
Nitrofurantoin achieves only urinary concentrations, not renal parenchymal levels — it is contraindicated for pyelonephritis and must never be used for upper UTI.
Oral trimethoprim is appropriate for uncomplicated lower UTI; it is insufficient for pyelonephritis with systemic upset requiring hospital admission and IV therapy.
Ureteric stent is indicated for obstructed infected kidney — ultrasound has excluded obstruction, so stent insertion is not warranted.
CT urogram is useful for suspected calculi but should not delay antibiotic administration in a systemically unwell patient with classic pyelonephritis — treat first.
clinicalPearl: Nitrofurantoin must NEVER be used for pyelonephritis — it does not achieve adequate renal tissue concentrations. Use IV cephalosporin or co-amoxiclav for upper UTI with systemic features.
keyClue: Fever + loin pain + leukocytes/nitrites + systemic features (tachycardia, tachypnoea) in a young woman = acute pyelonephritis requiring IV therapy.
commonExamTrap: Prescribing nitrofurantoin for upper UTI — it only treats the bladder, not the renal parenchyma.
missedConcept: The antibiotic choice for UTI must reflect anatomical location — nitrofurantoin is adequate for cystitis but fails in pyelonephritis due to poor tissue penetration.

clinicalStem: A 70-year-old man underwent transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) 2 hours ago for refractory BPH. He is now in recovery and reports confusion, nausea, and headache. The nursing staff note he appears restless. Observations: HR 58 bpm (bradycardic), BP 162/95 mmHg, SpO2 94%. Serum sodium returns as 118 mmol/L. He had no pre-operative electrolyte abnormalities. What complication has occurred and what is the priority treatment?
options: TURP syndrome (dilutional hyponatraemia from glycine irrigation absorption) — treat with fluid restriction and hypertonic saline (3% NaCl) with careful correction
Post-operative urinary sepsis — start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics immediately
Pulmonary embolism — therapeutic anticoagulation and CT pulmonary angiogram
Hypertensive emergency — IV labetalol to lower blood pressure urgently
Haemorrhagic stroke — urgent CT head and neurosurgery review
explanation: TURP syndrome results from absorption of large volumes of glycine (hypotonic) irrigation fluid through open venous sinuses during surgery, causing dilutional hyponatraemia. Symptoms include confusion, visual disturbances (glycine-specific), bradycardia, hypertension, nausea, and pulmonary oedema. Serum sodium 118 mmol/L confirms severe hyponatraemia. Treatment is fluid restriction, hypertonic saline (3% NaCl) to correct Na at ≤8–10 mmol/L per 24 hours (avoiding central pontine myelinolysis), and careful monitoring. Bipolar/laser TURP has largely replaced monopolar TURP to reduce this risk.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: TURP syndrome is the classic post-TURP complication; hypertonic saline corrects dilutional hyponatraemia but must be given slowly to avoid osmotic demyelination.
Sepsis could present post-operatively but would not cause acute hyponatraemia 118 mmol/L within 2 hours — the timing and biochemistry point clearly to TURP syndrome.
PE presents with tachycardia, pleuritic chest pain, and hypoxia — not bradycardia with hyponatraemia. This picture is not consistent with PE.
The hypertension is a consequence of the dilutional hyponatraemia and raised ICP — treating BP without correcting the underlying hyponatraemia is wrong and potentially harmful.
Confusion and headache could suggest stroke, but the Na of 118 mmol/L in the immediate post-TURP period is the defining clue — correct the electrolyte disturbance first.
clinicalPearl: TURP syndrome = post-TURP confusion + bradycardia + hypertension + hyponatraemia from glycine absorption. Correct sodium slowly with hypertonic saline to avoid osmotic demyelination.
keyClue: Confusion + bradycardia + hypertension + Na 118 mmol/L immediately after TURP = classic TURP syndrome.
commonExamTrap: Treating the hypertension in isolation — the BP rise is secondary to dilutional hyponatraemia and raised ICP; treating the root cause (sodium correction) is the priority.
missedConcept: TURP syndrome is caused by glycine irrigation absorption; bipolar/laser techniques have reduced but not eliminated this risk. Slow sodium correction prevents central pontine myelinolysis.
redFlagReason: Severe acute hyponatraemia (Na 118 mmol/L) with neurological features — risk of cerebral oedema, seizures, and death if not recognised and treated promptly.

clinicalStem: A 59-year-old woman with a 20-year history of smoking presents with painless macroscopic haematuria. Cystoscopy reveals a papillary bladder tumour; transurethral resection of bladder tumour (TURBT) is performed. Histology confirms: high-grade urothelial carcinoma, stage T1 (invades lamina propria), no muscle invasion, no carcinoma in situ (CIS). Staging CT shows no evidence of nodal or distant metastasis. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Repeat TURBT at 4–6 weeks plus intravesical BCG immunotherapy
Immediate radical cystectomy
Single intravesical mitomycin C instillation within 24 hours then discharge
External beam radiotherapy to the pelvis
Surveillance cystoscopy in 12 months only
explanation: High-grade T1 bladder cancer is a high-risk non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer (NMIBC). EAU guidelines recommend repeat TURBT at 4–6 weeks (to exclude understaged muscle-invasive disease and clear residual tumour) followed by a course of intravesical BCG (Bacillus Calmette-Guérin) immunotherapy to reduce recurrence and progression risk. Single mitomycin C is used for low-risk tumours. Radical cystectomy is reserved for BCG failure, T2+ disease, or CIS refractory to BCG.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: repeat TURBT ensures complete resection and accurate staging; intravesical BCG reduces recurrence and progression in high-grade T1 NMIBC.
Immediate radical cystectomy is premature for T1 (non-muscle-invasive) disease without muscle invasion — it is reserved for muscle-invasive disease (T2+) or BCG-refractory high-grade NMIBC.
Single intravesical mitomycin C is appropriate for low-risk (Ta, low-grade, solitary) bladder cancer — it is insufficient for high-grade T1 disease.
Radiotherapy is not the primary modality for NMIBC; it has a role as bladder-preservation therapy for muscle-invasive disease or in patients unfit for surgery.
Surveillance cystoscopy alone without intravesical therapy is inappropriate for high-risk NMIBC — BCG and repeat TURBT are required to reduce progression risk.
clinicalPearl: High-grade T1 bladder cancer = high-risk NMIBC: repeat TURBT at 4–6 weeks + intravesical BCG induction/maintenance. Radical cystectomy if BCG fails.
keyClue: High-grade T1 (lamina propria invasion only, no muscle invasion) = high-risk NMIBC requiring repeat TURBT + BCG, not immediate cystectomy.
commonExamTrap: Offering immediate radical cystectomy for T1 disease — muscle invasion (T2) is the threshold for radical surgery; T1 is managed with BCG after re-resection.
missedConcept: Repeat TURBT in high-grade T1 NMIBC is essential because up to 20–25% are upstaged to T2 (muscle-invasive) on re-resection, which fundamentally changes the management.
